titleEn: Sons of Hurthan bore the fledgling

verses.sadr: قَتلوا ابنَ عُروة َ ثمَّ لَطُّوا دُونَهُ
بَينَ ابنِ قُطْرَة َ وابنِ هاتِكٍ عَرْشِهِ

translationEn: The sons of Hurthan gave birth to the fledgling of Muharriq in the winding sands of al-Wadi'a with its barred gates. Do not give me drink by your hand unless I have seized flocks in a raid of many herds. Led by swift lean mares like the staffs of solitary travelers, and horses with loosened saddle-rings, their molars bared against the rocky heights. They burst through the dust-clouds with fierce looks beneath the swirling storms. When spears were leveled at their throats, they bared the sharp edges of their fangs. They bore the warriors of Ja'far — disheveled, like lions of the thicket — and armored men with clubs and keen-edged swords in their midst, protecting the flanks of their valley in the glory of the houses of Hajib and Shihab. O sons of Kilab, how can Ja'far be driven out while the sons of Dubayna camp at the watering places? They slew the son of 'Urwa and then blocked access to him until we took them before the great arbiter, between the son of Qutra and the son of the one who tore down his own roof — one who begrudges even a word to a visitor. These are a people whose excellence Ma'add has long acknowledged, and the truth is known to those of sound minds.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: swift, lean horse
fierce-faced, snarling
the battle, the clamor of war
fully armored warrior
clubs, heavy cudgels
sharp, cutting (of a sword)
minds, intellects (pl. of لُبّ)

context: This poem addresses the Banu Kilab tribe lamenting the expulsion of the Banu Ja'far, Labid's kinsmen, celebrating their martial valor and noble lineage. It was composed in the context of tribal disputes and raids in the pre-Islamic period.

titleEn: Usayma' circled the camels and stirred

verses.ajuz: لمْ تُمْسِ مِنِّي نَوْبًا وَلا قُرُبَا
ـنَ بالليلِ ومِنْ رَملِ عالجٍ كُشُبَا

verses.sadr: مِنْ بَعدِ ما جاوَزَتْ شَقائِقَ فالدّهـ
قَوْمي بَنُو عامِرٍ وَإنْ نَطَقَ الـ

translationEn: Usayma' circled the saddle-bags and her phantom image stirred longing within me — one of the daughters of the Banu Ja'far in their own land, who had not been close to me by turns or nearness. I feared no highland girl from Yemen, though we crossed many passes of juniper. The caravans passed Falaj, then the Hazn plateau, traveling by night through the sands of 'Alij with their knotted growth, after crossing the flint-plains of al-Dahn and the thick forests of al-Suman. The crowing of cocks and the striking of the monastery bell turned them away from their purpose, and they were avoided. Will a rugged, robust she-camel, cutting through the journey at full gallop, bring me to her abodes? She is like a wild cow at al-Ghumayr that has lost her calf at Kuthman. The poet then describes a magnificent rainstorm in vivid detail: lightning watched through the night, two floods meeting and contending, valleys overflowing, the south wind and north wind wrestling over the clouds, rains pouring upon drought-stricken lands for seasons. The poem closes with a wish that the rain's pasture may feed Usaym and her noble tribe, the Banu 'Amir — Labid's own tribe — whom no slander of enemies can diminish, for against such men the arrogant are silenced and the guardian of honor gives way.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: longing, ecstatic emotion
a strong, robust she-camel
a quick trotting pace
a young wild bull or calf
clouds moving low and heavy
became barren from drought
one who butts heads, an adversary

context: A long poem combining ghazal opening, journey description, and nature poetry culminating in a magnificent rainstorm — a hallmark of Labid's style. The poem was likely composed for the Banu 'Amir tribe in the Jahili period, showcasing Labid's mastery of desert geography and vivid natural imagery.

titleAr: أصْبَحْتُ أمْشي بَعْدَ سَلْمى بن مالكٍ
titleEn: I walk on after Salma son of Malik

verses.sadr: أصْبَحْتُ أمْشي بَعْدَ سَلْمى بن مالكٍ
يَضِجُّ إذا ظِلُّ الغُرابِ دَنَا لَهُ
وَبَعدَ أبي عمرٍو وذي الفضْلِ عامِرٍ

verses.ajuz: بهِ ذاتُ ظُفْرٍ لا تُوَرَّعُ باللَّجَبْ
أُتِيحَ لَهُ زَأوٌ فأُزْلِقَ عَنْ رَتَبْ

verses.sadr: ألَمْ تَرَ فيما يَذكُرُ النَاسُ أنّني
verses.ajuz: ذكرْتُ أبا لَيلى فأصبَحْتُ ذا أرَبْ

verses.sadr: فهوَّنَ ما ألْقَى وإنْ كُنْتُ مُثبتًا
verses.ajuz: يَقيني بأنْ لا حيَّ يَنجو من العَطَبْ

translationEn: I have come to walk in the morning after Salma ibn Malik, and after Abu Qays and 'Urwa, like a worn-out old camel that groans when the shadow of a crow falls upon it, dreading more injury to what remains of its spine and sinews. And after Abu 'Amr and the virtuous 'Amir of noble deeds, and after the hoped-for 'Urwa — the good one in times of distress. After Tufayl of great deeds, who was seized by the one with claws that no clamor can frighten away. And after Abu Hayyan on the day of Hamuma, when a crushing blow came upon him and he was swept from his lofty station. Do you not see from what the people recall that I remembered Abu Layla and woke with deep yearning? Yet what eases my grief, even as I am laid low, is my certainty that no living soul escapes ruin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a humped camel whose hump has been worn down
the vertebrae of the backbone
sinews, tendons
distresses, calamities (pl. of كُرْبة)
the one with claws — death personified
need, longing, purpose
destruction, ruin

context: This short elegy mourns several companions and tribal leaders who have passed away, each named by kunya (honorific title). It reflects Labid's recurring theme of being the last survivor of a noble generation, a sentiment intensified after his conversion to Islam.

titleAr: أرَى النّفسَ لَجّتْ في رَجاءٍ مُكذِّبِ
titleEn: I see the soul persisting in false hope

verses.sadr: أرَى النّفسَ لَجّتْ في رَجاءٍ مُكذِّبِ
وَأبّنْتُ مِنْ فَقْدِ ابنِ عَمٍّ وخُلَّةٍ
verses.ajuz: وفارَقتُ من عَمٍّ كريمٍ ومن أبِ

verses.sadr: جَميلِ الأُسَى فِيما أتَى الدَّهرُ دونَهُ
تَرَاهُ رَخيَّ البَالِ إنْ تَلْقَ تَلْقَهُ
verses.ajuz: كريمًا وما يَذّهَبْ بهِ الدَّهرُ يَذهبِ
ألا انْعَمْ على حُسنِ التحيّةِ واشَربِ

verses.sadr: لَدُنْ أنْ دعا ديكُ الصَّباحِ بسُحرَةٍ
فلَمْ أُسْدِ ما أرْعَى وتَبْلٍ رَدَدْتُهُ
verses.ajuz: وأنجَحْتُ بَعدَ اللّهِ من خيرِ مَطْلَبِ

verses.sadr: إذا ما نَأى منّي بَرَاحٌ نَفَضْتُهُ
verses.ajuz: وإنْ يدنُ مني الغَيبُ أُلجِمْ فأركَبِ
وألْقَتْ يَدًا في كافِرٍ مُسْيَ مَغرِبِ

verses.sadr: رِضَاكَ فإنْ تَضْرِبْ إذا مارَ عِطْفُهُ

verses.ajuz: إذا ما اسْجَهَرَّ الآلُ في كلّ سَبسَبِ

verses.sadr: متى ما أشأ أسْمَعْ عِرارًا بِقَفْرَة ٍ

verses.ajuz: وإنْ يُحزِنوا أركبْ بهم كلَّ مركَبِ

translationEn: I see the soul persisting in false hope, though it has already been tested — if only it would learn from experience. How many kings and common folk I have seen, how many noble delegations and cavalcades I have accompanied. I flattered the arrogant and climbed to him adorned in necklaces, stern-faced and scowling, yet parted from him with good praise behind his back. I lamented the loss of a cousin and a friend, and parted from noble uncles and fathers. They departed, leaving me nothing but hope for what lies ahead and yearning. Whatever time my death comes to me with a just measure of good deeds, I shall not be surprised. I am not a mountain like Aban or Saha, nor the eternal peaks of Sawaj and Ghurrab. I fulfilled needs and eased desires, for the young man's soul is pledged to the moonlit night of the cunning one. The poem describes a generous companion who entertains from dawn to the hour of the returning travelers, then transitions into vivid desert scenes: a rainstorm illuminating the landscape, the poet riding a sturdy camel through shimmering mirages, hunting scenes, and the resolving of disputes. He dismisses his adversaries' falsehoods and drives them apart like the scattered horns of a fallen herd — concluding that whether the path is easy or rugged, he will ride through all of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: persisted stubbornly, was insistent
the tested one, the experienced
commoners, subjects (as opposed to kings)
I flattered, appeased, was gentle with
I eulogized, I lamented the loss of
a captive, a prisoner
darkness, gloom of night

context: A long multi-themed qasida in which Labid reflects on his long life, the companions he has outlived, his generous companion, desert journeys by camel, nature scenes, and tribal disputes he resolved. Scholars regard it as one of his major poems reflecting both Jahili and early Islamic wisdom.

titleAr: قَضِّ اللُّبانَة َ لا أبَا لكَ واذْهَبِ
titleEn: Fulfill your purpose and go, fatherless one

verses.sadr: قَضِّ اللُّبانَة َ لا أبَا لكَ واذْهَبِ

translationEn: Fulfill your purpose, you fatherless one, and go — join your noble absent kinsmen. Those under whose shelter one lived have gone, and I remain among a lesser generation, like the hide of a mangy camel — who devour through excess and treachery, while the one who speaks truth among them is censured even if he raises no trouble. O Arbad, of good qualities and noble grandfathers — you left me to walk with a broken horn. Were it not for God, and the effort of the companion of Himyar, and my own exposure to every dark and difficult trial, your camels would have spent the summer in the highlands of the Hijaz, their milking herds grazing south of Nasifa toward the watering place of al-Haw'ab. I entered the tent of Khuwaymir disguised in his realm like a lion, and he rewarded me with a speaking scroll — a letter — and with gray cloaks whose opening sits at the shoulders. Truly, the great calamity, unlike any other, is the loss of every brother who shone like the light of a star.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: need, purpose, desire
flanks, shelter, protection (pl. of كَنَف)
a lesser successor generation
a broken horn — symbol of diminished strength
a parchment, a letter
a gray-colored cloak or garment
the great calamity, the grievous loss

context: This elegy was composed for Labid's brother Arbad, who was struck dead by lightning — an event the tradition records as divine punishment for Arbad's hostility toward the Prophet Muhammad. The poem opens dramatically, as Labid addresses himself or a companion, and closes with one of his most famous lines about the incomparable grief of losing a star-like brother.

titleEn: The heart grew restless, would it had not

verses.ajuz: إنَّ الغويَّ إذا نُهي لمْ يُعْتِبِ

verses.sadr: فتَعَزَّ عَنْ هذا وَقُلْ في غَيرِهِ

translationEn: The heart grew restless — would it had not grown so — and the memory of a distant companion weighed upon it. Foolishness — had I heeded my blamers when they pointed toward the slope of the valley, I would have restrained a heart that heeds no restrainer, for the misguided one, when forbidden, does not relent. Compose yourself, set this aside, and speak of other things; remember the noble character of your noble brother. O Arbad of goodness, whose grandfathers were noble — you left me walking with a broken horn, alone. The great calamity, unlike any calamity, is the loss of every brother who shone like the light of a star. Those in whose shelter one lived have gone, and I remain among a lesser generation like the mangy camel's hide, who devour through greed and treachery while the honest speaker is censured. And yet I see myself at times as one of the Ja'far clan, blessed as by a copious downpour — men like sharp lance-tips, proud chieftains as difficult to lead as an unbroken camel-stallion, a people whose fathers set for them noble customs, and glory that comes without even being sought. Now their loss has carved through my flesh to the very bones, and Time, if I reproach it, gives no answer.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: became restless with longing, was stirred emotionally
a close companion, intimate friend
to be near, to be close
the blamers, female reproachers
the erring one, the misguided
the prize camel-stallion, unbroken and proud
whittled away, wore down gradually

context: A second elegy for Arbad, Labid's brother, which shares lines with poem 05 but expands with reflections on the degeneracy of the current generation contrasted with the nobility of the Ja'far clan. The repeated refrain about Arbad's death as 'the great calamity' underscores how deeply this loss shaped Labid's poetic consciousness.

titleEn: We spent the night where evening found us
translationEn: We spent the night where evening found us, nearby upon a hard-packed earth where the dogs barked at us. We transferred their captives, group by group, to another group — as a share of the lot is passed along. We were angered by what the Nufayl tribe suffered, and the best seeker of blood-vengeance is the one who burns with anger. We brought the horses — lean from the two leanest seasons — streaming along, whipped by the morning cold.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hard-packed, flat ground
dogs (collective)
a small group, a camp unit
a share, a lot divided among people
blood revenge, vendetta
lean, emaciated (pl. of أعجف)
the morning frost, early cold

context: A short fragment on a tribal raid undertaken in vengeance for wrongs suffered by the Nufayl group. The poem celebrates the warrior's resolve and the disciplined movement of raiders who travel by night and transfer captives in organized groups.

titleEn: I praised God, and God is the Praised
translationEn: I praise God — and God is the Praised One, to Whom belong all established abundance and multitude. For the grace of God is a gift that only the pious can attain, and only the blessed can truly claim it. I am not as Abu Hufayd describes me, nor like his weak and dull drinking-companion. My paternal uncle is the son of generosity and Abu Shuraih, and my uncle Khalid is wisdom and open-handedness. My grandfather was the horseman of the trembling mare — a chief of true substance, neither a coward nor a follower. My maternal uncle was honored in the settled villages and received more than delegations are given. I found my father to be a spring of generosity to orphans and guests when the roasted meat was most desired. My uncles are Khidyam, Abu Zuhayr, Zanbaq, and their patron Asid — and Qays, the clan of the family of Abu Usaym. Measure them if you wish and see what you gain. These are my kinsmen; match them if you can, for in your two branches there is none equal to them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: deeply rooted wealth and glory
an extra gift, a bonus beyond obligation
a drinking companion
firmness, wisdom, decisiveness
the trembling she-camel — a famous horse name
delegations, visiting groups
roasted meat (a sign of generous hospitality)

context: A poem of tribal pride (fakhr) in which Labid opens with praise of God before cataloging his noble ancestry — paternal uncles, maternal uncles, grandfather, and father — each celebrated for generosity, wisdom, and leadership. The poem challenges an unnamed rival to match his lineage.

titleEn: Affairs are settled and the promise fulfilled

verses.sadr: ما إنْ أهابُ إذا السُّرادِقُ غَمَّهُ

translationEn: Affairs have been decided and the promise of God has been fulfilled — my Lord is glorious and ever praised. To Him belong all gifts, all bounties, all heights, and the dense wealth of goodness that can be counted. The people of Iram and 'Ad tested His power, and after them Thamud tested it too — they left their garments lying on their private places, and now they lie motionless in the courtyards of their homes. I have grown weary of life and its length, and of people asking: how is Labid? I lived through a full age, even before the famous war of Dahis — had the stubborn soul any eternity. I witnessed the confidential assemblies of al-Ufaqa standing tall, my rank elevated, with the retinues of kings as my witnesses. Your father Busr's long age cannot be reproached — yet everything new must eventually decay. A long and endless time has overcome my patience though I was not one to be overcome. Each day that passes over me and each night — both return after they have gone, and I see time coming again as it came the first day I encountered it: unbroken in its vigor while I have grown weak. Yet I defended my people when the 'Amir tribe called on me, and I stepped forward on the day of al-Ghabit when delegations thronged. The pillars of every tribe pressed forward while the horsemen of the mighty king held them back. I kept my honor from being reached by any whispered slander — for the one innocent of wrongs is truly blessed. I do not fear when the pavilion fills with gloom and the clashing of bows makes the coward tremble.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: favors, surpluses of generosity
dense, abundant (of vegetation or goodness)
lying motionless, extinguished (of embers or people)
faults, moral slips, indiscretions
a large tent or canopy, a pavilion
a coward who trembles with fear
a private conversation, whispered slander

context: A poem of Islamic wisdom and late-life reflection, mentioning ancient destroyed nations (Iram, 'Ad, Thamud) as evidence of divine power — a theme Labid embraced after converting to Islam. He balances this with praise of his own honor and tribal defense, showing the blend of Jahili and Islamic values in his late poetry.

titleAr: ما إنْ تُعَرّي المَنُونُ مِنْ أحَدِ
titleEn: Death strips no one bare of its reach

verses.sadr: ما إنْ تُعَرّي المَنُونُ مِنْ أحَدِ
يا عَينُ هَلَاّ بَكَيْتِ أرْبَدَ إذْ

translationEn: Death strips no one bare — not a compassionate parent, not a child. I feared misfortune for Arbad and yet did not dread the rainstars of al-Simak or Leo. Then thunder and lightning bereaved me of the horseman, the brave one on the day of bitter battle — the warrior who healed the plundered when they came wounded, and would return to help again. He forgave in times of hardship and begging as freely as the spring rain descends in its appointed time. He did not allow his eye to reach all its satisfaction on the night when even the fine horses stood like strips of dried meat. All children of free women — their end is diminishment, however large their numbers. If they are envied, they are humbled; if they flourish for a day, they are brought to ruin and misery. O my eye, why did you not weep for Arbad when we rose and the adversaries rose in the heat of dispute? And again, why did you not weep for Arbad when the winter winds swept away the branches? The tree became pregnant and was trimmed when the remaining days had passed. If they raised a clamor Arbad would not regard it; if they sought fairness in judgment he would be measured. He was sweet and noble — yet in his sweetness there was a bitter edge for those who wronged, tender in heart and liver. He was the one whose funeral roused lament — mourning women rising like young gazelles in open ground.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: death, fate (feminine noun)
sudden calamities, fatal strokes
a rainstorm associated with a specific star's rising
one who comes wounded and stripped of property
full satisfaction of desire, appetite
strips of dried meat — metaphor for emaciation
funeral gatherings, mourning assemblies

context: The title comes from the refrain 'O eye, why did you not weep for Arbad' — an elegy for Labid's brother Arbad ibn Rabi'a. The poem philosophically opens with universal mortality before moving to direct lament, celebrating Arbad's courage, generosity, and judicial temperament.

titleEn: You two shall never exhaust Arbad's goodness

verses.ajuz: ـةِ إذْ رَأى أنْ لا خُلُودَا

translationEn: You two will never exhaust Arbad's goodness, so weep until he returns. Say: he was the hero who defended us when men were clad in iron armor. He turned away the wrongdoers when we met the proud and mighty enemy. But the uncertainties of creation seized him when he saw there was no eternity. He settled in his grave — feeling no pain, suffering no illness — and he was the one who was truly missed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the hero, the brave warrior
the defender, the one who protects
proud, stiff-necked (pl. of أصيد — describing proud enemies)
the uncertainties of creation, the vicissitudes of life
hindered him, held him back — here: death took him
the one who is lost, the missed one
to suffer illness or weariness

context: A short lyrical elegy in the rajaz-derived mujazza' al-kamil meter, composed for Arbad. Addressed to two mourners (likely Labid's daughters or unnamed companions), it ends on the distinctive note that Arbad died without suffering — a consolation for his survivors.

titleEn: The tribe departed from Hajar after the early start

verses.sadr: مَنْأى الفَرُورِ فَما يأتي المُريدَ ومَا
verses.ajuz: يَسْلُو الصُّدودَ إذا ما كانَ يَقتَدِرُ
طَلحُ السَّلائلِ وَسطَ الرَّوْضِ أوْ عُشَرُ

verses.sadr: كأنَّ فاها إذا ما اللّيلُ ألْبَسهَا
verses.ajuz: سَيَابَةٌ ما بِها عَيْبٌ ولا أثَرُ
أنتَ الذي كنتَ، لوْلا الشّيبُ وَالكِبرُ

verses.sadr: فقلتُ: ليسَ بَياضُ الرَّأسِ من كِبرٍ
لوْ كانَ غيري، سليمى، اليوْمَ غَيَّرَهُ
ما يَمنعُ اللّيْلُ مِنّي ما هَمَمْتُ بِهِ
verses.ajuz: وَلا أحارُ إذا ما اعتادَني السَّفَرُ

verses.sadr: إنَي أُقاسي خُطوبًا ما يَقُومُ لَهَا
مِن فَقدِ مولىً تَصُورُ الحيَّ جَفنَتُهُ
verses.ajuz: أوْ رُزْء مالٍ، ورُزْءُ المالِ يُجْتَبَرُ

verses.sadr: والنِّيبُ، إنْ تَعْرُ مِنّي رمَّة ً خَلَقًا
ولا أقولُ إذا ما أزْمَة ٌ أزَمَتْ
verses.ajuz: يا وَيْحَ نَفْسيَ مِمّا أحدَثَ القدَرُ

verses.sadr: إنْ يُتْلِفوا يُخلِفوا في كلِّ مَنْقَصًة ٍ
verses.ajuz: ما أتلَفوا، لابتغاء الحَمدِ، أوْ عَقَرُوا
فمَا يُحَسُّ بهِ عَينٌ ولا أثَرُ
في نَفْسِها مِنْ حَبيبٍ فاقِدٍ ذِكَرُ
فأقْبَلَتْ ما بِها رَوْعٌ وَلا بَهَرُ

translationEn: The tribe departed from Hajar after setting out early in the day, and Salma neither maintains contact nor abandons it. She is distant like the one who flees — neither coming to the one who desires her nor growing indifferent when she can. Their litters in the morning light resemble acacias of the valleys amid the meadow, or white euphorbia. Or the cool summer palms with dark leafy tops, richly endowed by Hajar — short date-clusters and tall trunks bending beneath the weight of budding fruit, unveiled and stripped. They drink freely and in rivalry, never departing — every one of them wading deep in the water. Between al-Safa and the eye of the spring, tall trees stand bowing without embarrassment. In the litters rides a coquettish woman of good character, full in figure, dazzling to the eye. Her mouth in the night's covering is like an unblemished, unscratched citrus fruit. She said to me in the morning of our secret talk by her companion: 'You are as you were, were it not for grey hair and age.' I replied: 'White hair does not come from old age if you but knew — the knowing one has the information. Had it been someone else, Salma, the blows of time would have changed him — all but the sharp sword of resolve.' Night does not prevent me from what I intend, nor do I grow confused when travel becomes my habit. I endure calamities that none can bear but the noble who are patient with their like — from the loss of a patron whose bowl sustained the tribe, or the loss of wealth, and loss of wealth can be recovered. The old she-camels, if they find my old bones after my death, they will know I was one who sought revenge and was not stingy with the gift of the hump-meat when the odor of roasting rose like incense. I do not cry woe to my soul when drought strikes. I do not lose companions I guide through the darkness when the road spreads out in the night. A long narrative follows of a she-ostrich who digs a nest all night at the root of a tamarisk tree, is destroyed by a hunter at dawn with his keen dogs — she flees but is caught by the foremost of her pursuers, then turns and fights back: for the defender, after fear strikes, always turns and fights.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the tribe, the settled community departing together
litters on camels, traveling women in palanquins
a woman who is loving and coquettish toward her husband
the haunches, buttocks (of a woman — indicating full figure)
a vast flat plain that seems endless
the male ostrich
turned back and fought fiercely, wheeled around

context: One of Labid's longest and most structurally complete qasidas in al-Basit meter, following the classical tripartite structure: nasib (love prelude), self-praise with virtues, and an extended wasf (description) of the camel journey culminating in the celebrated she-ostrich hunting scene — a masterpiece of Jahili nature poetry.

titleAr: وَلمْ تَحْمَ عَبدُ اللّهِ، لا درَّ دَرُّها،
titleEn: Banu Abdallah did not defend, curse them

verses.sadr: وَلمْ تَحْمَ عَبدُ اللّهِ، لا درَّ دَرُّها،
verses.ajuz: على خيرِ قَتْلاها، ولم تَحْمَ جَعفرُ

verses.sadr: وَدَوْكُمْ غَضَا الوادي فلم تَكُ دِمنة ٌ

translationEn: The Banu 'Abdallah did not defend — may their yield be cursed — over the best of those they slew, and Ja'far did not defend either. The children of al-Dibab did not defend — as if young she-camels were being driven through the middle of the stripped grove among them. You trampled the tamarisk wood of the valley and left neither lasting mark nor blood-feud that a rememberer would pursue. Is it true that you never defended for all of time even a hollow in the ground, the way Mubashir defended the breadth of the Hijaz? How quickly would the people have handed me your spoils by force — that is the shameful custom, and the lance-thrust brings one back to their senses. What a difference between war and being forced to submit in humiliation, while the degraded and driven one accepts subjugation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may her milk dry up — a curse expression
a strip of land stripped of trees, a clear cut area
a lasting trace, the mark of a former camp
a hollow or watercourse in the ground
by force, compulsorily
oppression, injustice, subjugation
the driven one, the one herded like cattle

context: A short poem of tribal reproach (hijaa' blended with fakhr), attacking the Banu 'Abdallah and related clans for their failure to defend their slain and to uphold blood-vengeance — contrasted with the noble tradition of defense represented by Mubashir.

titleAr: يا بشْرُ بشرَ بَني إيَادٍ أيُّكُمْ
titleEn: O Bishr, bearer of tidings of Iyad

verses.sadr: يا بشْرُ بشرَ بَني إيَادٍ أيُّكُمْ

translationEn: O Bishr, bearer of news for the Banu Iyad — which one of you returned Urayka on the day of the highland of al-Ajshur? Boys were riding tandem upon her spine with the comfort of a trusted mount toward the spear-points of the watering place. She came bearing a saddle-frame and a water-skin pack, and you relieved her of the struggle of the tightened girth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bearer of good news, announcer
a high rocky plateau, a highland
the vertebral column, the spine
riding behind another — tandem riding
a wooden saddle-frame for camels
a large water-skin carried on a camel
the girth-rope of a pack saddle

context: A very short fragment or complete miniature poem addressed to a man named Bishr from the Banu Iyad, demanding to know which of them returned the camel Urayka after a tribal encounter at the highland of al-Ajshur — possibly relating to a debt of honor or a raid.

titleEn: Whoever among you was ignorant of me

verses.ajuz: فَما كانَ بِدْعًا مِنْ بَلائيَ عامِرُ

verses.sadr: ودافعتُ عنكَ الصّيدَ مِن آلِ دارمٍ
فُقَيْمٌ وعَبْدُ اللهِ في عِزِّ نَهْشَلٍ
على حينَ مَنْ تَلْبَثْ عَلَيهِ ذَنُوبُهُ
وفي كلِّ يومٍ ذي حفاظٍ بَلَوتَني
وما يكُ منْ شيءٍ فقدْ رُعتَ رَوْعة ً
فلَوْ كانَ مَوْلايَ أمرَأً ذا حَفِيظَةٍ
verses.ajuz: إذًا زَفَّ راعي البَهْمِ والبَهمُ نافِرُ
منَ الأرْضِ إلَاّ حيثُ تُبغى الجَعافرُ
قَريبٌ، إذا ما صَدَّ عَنّي المَعَاشِرُ
يَصِرْ مَعْقِلَ الحَقِّ الذي هوَ صَائِرُ

translationEn: Whoever among you was ignorant of me or deceived — the trials of 'Amir with me were no novelty. I kept your company until the sons of Umm al-Banin, the elders, harbored suspicion against me. I defended you from the proud ones of the house of Darim, among whom was a clan boasting under silken pavilions. Fuqaym and 'Abdallah stood in the glory of the Nahshal at Thaytala — all present and mutually supporting. I drove off Ma'add, the 'Ibad, Tayyi', and Kalb as young camels are driven at dawn. At a time when whoever delays his load will find its absence keenly felt, and in the trailing group there is tardiness. I led Rabi' to the encampment proud as a breeding-stallion of pure white camels, seeking a challenger — then silenced him until he submitted like a worn knife scraping weakly in slow strokes. And on the day you departed and your delegations climbed the rocky plateaus of Fathur — I stood with noble patience. On the day I prevented the tribe from scattering at Najran — that day broke my very back. And on a day in the plain of al-Ghabit with kings and their attendants as my witnesses, in every day of honor you tested me and I stood where the base ones cannot. The victory belongs to me from them, and the bond of loyalty upon you — I was no lowly truffle that flat valleys produce. You are destitute and have found no replacement for me, nor have your youngest sons matched up. I said: restrain the inclinations of your omen-bird and know that if you step forward, you will stumble. For a neighbor's humiliation of a neighbor is painful — a disaster that draws other disasters to itself. Whichever way you face it you will be miserable, with both its mounts unstable under your feet. If you advance, you encounter a great obstacle ahead; if you retreat, the rear is treacherous. Whatever happens, you have struck terror, O Abu Malik — terror that turns braids white. Had my protector been a man of true honor, the shepherd of young camels would have hurried away and the lambs would have scattered. Do not seek me out when you have seized a herded prize from this land except where the Ja'far are sought. They are closest to me in loyalty, and their support is near when all other companions turn away. When my horses chase behind my captured prize, the fortress of right becomes what it always was destined to be — and I rallied them after their scattering, and they became a lovely, well-formed troop before Ibn Asid.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one deceived or inexperienced, kept in the dark
the proud, those with stiff necks — a noble clan epithet
a herd of captured livestock driven off in a raid
a truffle that grows in low, worthless ground — metaphor for lowborn
calamities that break one's back (pl. of فاقرة)
braided locks of hair
the Banu Ja'far — Labid's tribe

context: A long poem of self-defense and tribal boasting directed at a patron or tribal chief who has treated Labid poorly. Labid catalogs his services — battles fought, enemies repelled, the tribe held together — and warns his addressee of the consequences of losing his loyalty. The poem is a masterwork of the fakhr genre.

titleAr: أعاذِلَ قُومي فاعذُلي الآنَ أوْ ذَري
titleEn: O blame me now or leave it, blamer

verses.sadr: أعاذِلَ قُومي فاعذُلي الآنَ أوْ ذَري
أعاذِلَ لا وَاللهِ ما مِنْ سَلامَةٍ

verses.ajuz: بهِ الحَمدَ إنَّ الطّالبَ الحمدَ مُشترِي

verses.sadr: وكَمْ مُشترٍ من مالِهِ حُسنَ صِيِتهِ
أُباهي بهِ الأكْفاءَ في كلِّ مَوْطِنٍ
وَلا منْ أبي جَزْءٍ وجارَيْ حَمُومَةٍ
وقَيسِ بنِ جَزْءٍ يَوْمَ نادى صِحابَهُ

verses.ajuz: إذا ما الكَعَابُ أصبحتْ لم تَسَتَّرِ

verses.sadr: ومَنْ كانَ أهلَ الجودِ والحزْمِ والندى

verses.ajuz: متى يَدْعُ مَوْلَاهُ إلى النّصرِ يُنْصَرِ

verses.sadr: فلَمْ أَرَ يَوْمًا كانَ أكْثَرَ باكِيًا

verses.ajuz: شُجاعٌ وذو عَقْدٍ منَ القَوْمِ مُحتَرِ

verses.sadr: أُولئِكَ فابكي لا أبَا لَكِ وانْدُبي
verses.ajuz: أبَا حازِمٍ في كُلِّ يَوْمٍ مُذَكَّرِ
فهَل بَعْدَهُمْ مِنْ خالدٍ أوْ مُعَمَّرِ
وما إنْ لَنا مِنْ سادَة ٍ غير حِميرِ

translationEn: O blamer, rise up and blame me now or leave it — I shall not cease even if you cease. By God, there is no safety, even if the soul of the miserly accumulator were to be cautious. I protect my honor with old inherited wealth and buy praise with it — the seeker of praise is indeed a buyer. How many have bought fine reputation with their wealth, their days honored in every encampment and stopping-place. I take pride before my peers at every station, fulfilling the obligations of the righteous and earning additional merit. Though you see me safe among you today, I am no more alive than the men of Kilab and Ja'far, or Abu Jaz' and the two companions of Hamuma — their slain one and the one who drank deeply drop by drop — nor than the two Ahwas who went in succession, nor the companion of al-Barrad who was never deceived. And not more living than Rabi' the generous, of whom I was bereaved at Dhu 'Alaq — so gain yourself patience and preserve your dignity. The poem then catalogs fallen heroes one by one, mourning their absence, describing the weeping of beautiful women, the lamentations at graves, asking: after all the grey-beards and young men of the sky-water tribe, is there a single immortal or long-liver? They went as predecessors, the road clear before them. How many scenes of splendor and beauty I have witnessed, how many prisoners freed from their chains. Time destroyed the lords of Na'it at their high vantage point, struck down the warlike al-Harith devastating his people, slew the lord of Kinda and his son, the master of Ma'add between the desert-flat and the summit, outmaneuvered the lord of al-Dumayy from his fortress top, and brought down the lord of al-Mushaqqar by its ropes. It deceived Quss and baffled Luqman's wisdom of deliberation. If you ask what we are — we are sparrows of this bewildered humanity, slaves to the living Himyar if they rule us, wronged by the agents of Chosroes and Caesar. We and they together belong as subjects to Himyar by force — we have no masters but Himyar. Seventy Tubba' kings before the last Tubba' succeeded one another in splendor. We settle lands all of which were settled before us, and hope for salvation after 'Ad and Himyar. We and our brothers who followed one after another are like the one departing at dawn and the one returning at dusk — is the soul anything but a borrowed pleasure, lent out only to return to its Lord after a brief span of months?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: old inherited wealth, ancestral property
reputation, fame (that spreads by sound)
the obligatory duties of righteous people
a young woman just becoming fully grown
the kings of Yemen (Tubba' — sing. Tubba')
salvation, ultimate success
borrowed, lent (describing the soul as a loan from God)

context: One of Labid's greatest philosophical qasidas, blending self-praise, elegy for fallen heroes, historical meditation on destroyed civilizations, and a profound closing statement on the soul as a borrowed pleasure. This poem reflects Labid's Islamic sensibility merged with pre-Islamic fatalism, making it one of the finest examples of transitional Jahili-Islamic poetry.

titleEn: By my life if the informant spoke truly

verses.ajuz: لَقَدْ رُزِئَتْ في سالِفِ الدَّهرِ جعفَرُ

verses.sadr: فتىً كانَ؛ أمّا كُلَّ شيء سألْتَهُ
فإنْ يَكُ نَوءٌ مِنْ سَحابٍ أصابَهُ
verses.ajuz: فقَد كانَ يَعلُو في اللِّقاءِ ويظفَرُ

translationEn: By my life, if the informant spoke truthfully, then the Ja'far tribe has suffered a grave loss in times past. He was a young man — whatever you asked of him, he gave, and every fault he would forgive. If a cloud's rainstorm struck him down, he had been one who rose high in battle and triumphed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: by my life — an oath formula
the informant, the one who brings news
suffered a loss, was bereaved (passive)
in bygone times, in the past ages
a rainstorm or thunderstorm tied to a star's rising
to triumph, to be victorious
to rise high, to be superior in battle

context: A brief three-verse elegy for a member of the Ja'far clan — possibly connected to the death of a prominent warrior. The poem turns on a beautiful ambiguity in the closing verse: the 'rainstorm' that struck the hero may be the lightning that also killed Labid's brother Arbad, or a metaphor for death generally.

titleEn: Every fierce adversary reminds me of Arbad
translationEn: Every fierce adversary reminds me of Arbad — one so dogged in opposition you would think his very scheme is meant to harm. If they sought moderation he was a moderate and shrewd man; if they deviated from the path of right, he too would swerve. He would guide the people, strong-backed and sure, when the tribe's own leader was lost in the desert.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one extremely stubborn in dispute, fiercely contentious
a scheme, a proposed plan of action
to be moderate, to follow the middle course
shrewd, intelligent, prudent
strong-backed, capable of bearing a heavy burden
a vast empty desert, a featureless wilderness
to be bewildered, to lose one's way

context: A short elegy for Arbad in which Labid reflects on how every legal dispute he faces now reminds him of his brother, who was a paragon of moderation, intelligence, and leadership in difficult situations. The poem is notable for praising Arbad's wisdom in legal matters.

titleEn: I weep for Abu al-Hazzaz at the gathering
translationEn: I weep for Abu al-Hazzaz on a day of tribal assembly — at the resting place of guests and the shelter of those in straitened means. For the tribe, when winter came upon them early and a Syrian cloud-wind promised a moonlit cold day. The miserly had wrapped themselves in their rooms and the gambling-arrow lot-casters divided up every prized animal. You found Arbad there, his face shining like a full moon — generous, never hoarding, never selfish.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place of standing, a tribal assembly or gathering
a resting place, a place where camels kneel
the miserly, those who refuse to contribute to a feast
participants in the maysir arrow-gambling game
a famous prized animal slaughtered for a feast
one who is stingy, who keeps a tight grip on resources
one who takes all for himself, selfish

context: A four-verse elegy for Arbad (called Abu al-Hazzaz by kunya) set in the specific social context of a winter tribal gathering, where the contrast between the miserly who hide in their tents and Arbad's luminous generosity is beautifully drawn with the simile of the full moon.

titleEn: Only the righteous truly guard piety

verses.sadr: يَوْمَ لا يُدخِلُ المُدارِسَ في الرَّحـ
إنْ يكنْ في الحَياةِ خَيرٌ فقد أُنْـ
عِشْتُ دَهْرًا ولا يَدومُ على الأيـ
لمْ يُهينوا الموْلى على حدَثِ الدّهْـ

translationEn: Only the righteous truly guard piety, and all final rest returns to God. To God you shall be returned, and with God lies the incoming and the outgoing of all affairs. Everything He has counted in a book and by knowledge, and with Him all secrets are revealed. On the day when the provisions of those He favors are laden camels and milking she-camels with heavy udders, rising to their humps with abundance, while the tall palms and the Jabbar trees ripen — on a day when only repentance and asking forgiveness will bring the sinner into mercy, and beautiful things prepared for those who gave testimony, and the forgiveness of the All-Forgiving — and what a station of honor, and what a guide, a straight path and a counsel. If there is good in life then I have been given time — though time gained is of little use. I lived a long age and nothing endures through the days except the mountains Yararmam and Ti'ar — and Kulaf and Dalfa' and Badi' and what stands above Khubba: Timar. And the stars that follow one another through the night, veering to the right — their motion constant, as the low-setting star turns them the way a nursing she-camel is turned by the milking women. Then vision fails when they are hidden from us — one cannot tell if their ropes are long or short. The 'Amir tribe is gone, and nothing remains of them in the meadows of al-A'raf except ruins — only mirages, thorny fences, and shade-shelters that winds and rains have scattered. I see the clan of 'Amir has bid me farewell — all but a people whose horses are colts, standing guard at every feared frontier — by my grandfather's life, they are pure gold. They never humiliated a patron when time turned against him, and in-laws did not find them tiresome. So upon 'Amir be peace and all praise, wherever they have settled or journeyed in the land.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: piety, fear of God, God-consciousness
incoming and outgoing — used metaphorically for all affairs referred to God
a tall proud palm tree — also one of God's names
one who has committed sins, one who needs erasure of wrongs
she-camels who suckle young — used in the star metaphor
young untrained colts, horse foals
pure gold — used as a compliment for the finest warriors

context: A poem composed after Labid's conversion to Islam, opening with explicitly Quranic themes of divine judgment, the Day of Reckoning, and divine knowledge of all things. It then transitions to reflections on the passing of time, the endurance of mountains versus tribes, and closes with praise of the 'Amir tribe — showcasing Labid's unique blend of Islamic piety and tribal loyalty.

titleEn: My daughters wished their father would live on

verses.ajuz: وهَلْ أنا إلَاّ من رَبيعَةَ أوْ مُضَرْ
أخا ثِقَةٍ لا عَينَ مِنهُ ولا أثَرْ
وَلا تَخْمِشَا وَجْهًا وَلا تحْلِقا شَعَرْ
أضاعَ، وَلا خانَ الصَّديقَ وَلا غَدَرْ

verses.sadr: إلى الحَوْلِ ثمَّ اسمُ السّلَامِ علَيكُما
verses.ajuz: وَمَنْ يَبْكِ حَوْلًا كاملًا فقدِ اعتذرْ

translationEn: My two daughters wish that their father might live on — but am I not simply one of the men of Rabi'a or Mudar, subject to the same mortality as all? Two weeping women lament at the highland of 'Aqil a trustworthy brother of whom no trace nor sign remains. You have consolation in the example of the two sons of Nizar if you grieve — ask them and you shall be told of those who went before. And in all who were kings or commoners beyond them — pillars of a throne that time betrayed and uprooted. Rise up and say what you know of him — do not scratch your faces or shave your heads. Say: he was a man who never squandered a friendship, never betrayed a companion, never acted treacherously. Until the year's end — and then peace be upon you both: for whoever weeps a full year has fulfilled all obligation of grief.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to wish, to long for
a professional or personal mourner, a lamenting woman
a consolation, an example to follow in grief
to scratch the face as a sign of mourning
betrayal, treachery
a full year
pillars of power — those who support a kingdom

context: A poem addressed to Labid's own daughters urging them not to engage in prohibited mourning practices (scratching faces, shaving hair) and to instead testify to his good character. The final verse — that weeping a full year excuses one from further grief — became one of the most famous lines in Arabic poetry and is cited in classical literary sources.

titleAr: دَعي اللَّوْمَ أوْ بِيني كشِقِّ صَديعِ
titleEn: Leave off blame or depart like a split crack

verses.sadr: دَعي اللَّوْمَ أوْ بِيني كشِقِّ صَديعِ
verses.ajuz: فقَدْ لُمْتِ قَبلَ اليَوْمِ غيرَ مُطيعِ
ومِنْ قَبْلُ قَد قَوَّمْتُ دَرْءَ رَبيعِ

translationEn: Leave off blaming me or depart, like the split of a cracked stone — you have blamed me before and I was no more obedient then. If you desire separation, then separate — whether the affair ends in dispersal or in reunion. Had I accumulated my wealth and its offspring and held it as tightly as a miser's grip, you would have been satisfied with the lowest level of our living and praised us when you returned from sour milk and a soaking drink. But my wealth is consumed by every great bowl — when it is time for the watering, it flows out in tears of generosity. And by giving to my patron at the moment of his poverty when he says: look at my need and my humility. And adversaries like a conclave of spirits — I threw them down with a firm and twisting grip, like the adversaries of the Banu Badr on the morning I met them — and before that I had already straightened the resistance of Rabi'.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a deeply cracked stone or object
dispersal, separation, scattering apart
to make wealth grow, to multiply wealth
sour milk — a basic, humble food
a soaking drink of water and dates or barley
a large wooden bowl used for feeding guests
wrestling throws, the act of bringing an opponent down

context: A poem of self-justification addressed to a blaming wife or companion, in which Labid defends his generosity as the cause of his poverty, presenting it as a noble choice rather than a flaw. The poem exemplifies the Jahili concept of honor through giving, where wealth exists to be shared rather than hoarded.

titleEn: We waste away while the rising stars do not

verses.sadr: وَقَد كنتُ في أكنافِ جارِ مَضِنّةٍ
فَلا جَزِعٌ إنْ فَرَّقَ الدَّهْرُ بَيْنَنا
verses.ajuz: وكُلُّ فَتى ً يَوْمًَا بهِ الدَّهْرُ فاجِعُ
وَلا أنا مِمّا أحدَثَ الدَّهرُ جازِعُ
يَحُورُ رَمادًا بَعْدَ إذْ هُوَ سَاطِعُ

verses.sadr: ومَا البِرُّ إلَاّ مُضْمَراتٌ منَ التُّقَى

verses.ajuz: إذا ارْتَحَلَ الفِتْيَانُ مَنْ هوَ راجعُ

verses.sadr: تُبَكِّي على إثرِ الشّبابِ الذي مَضَى

verses.ajuz: وَلا زاجِراتُ الطّيرِ ما اللّهُ صانِعُ
يَذُوقُ المَنَايا أوْ متى الغَيثُ واقِعُ

translationEn: We wear away while the rising stars do not wear away, and the mountains and fortresses remain after us. I had been under the shelter of a protector I could count on, but a companion of Arbad's usefulness parted from me. No grief for that — when time separates us: every young man is one day struck by time. No joyful novelty comes to me, nor do I grieve at what time brings newly about. People are like abandoned campsites and their inhabitants — they dwell there on the day they settle and by morning the place is empty. A man is like a shooting star and its light — it turns to ashes after its brilliance. Righteousness is nothing but inward piety held secret, and wealth is nothing but deposits held temporarily for others. Wealth and family are merely a trust — and one day, inevitably, trusts are returned. They depart in successive waves and we follow after them, as a following camel is gathered by the lead camel ahead. People are only two kinds of workers: one who ruins what he builds, and another who raises. Among them the happy one takes his share, and the wretched one is content with mere existence. Is there not waiting behind me, should death be delayed, the clinging to a staff upon which fingers curve? I tell the stories of generations that have passed, stooped over — as if every time I rise I am bowing down. I have become like a sword whose scabbard time and the blacksmith's long-ago labor have aged — yet the blade remains sharp. So do not distance yourself — for death has an appointment with you, and it draws near, rising to meet you. O blamer, what do you know — except by guessing — when the young men ride out, who among them shall return? You weep over the trail of youth that has passed — know that the companions of youth are the frivolous ones. Do you grieve at what time does to the young man? What nobleman has not been struck by the hammer-blows of fate? By your life, the diviners who cast pebbles and the omen-readers of birds do not know what God will do — ask them, if you doubt me, when the young man will taste death or when the rains will fall.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we wore away, decayed, grew old (from بَلِيَ)
constructed fortresses, permanent buildings
a shooting star — used as a metaphor for fleeting life
deposits, things entrusted to one's care temporarily
crushing calamities, hammer-blows of fate
the frivolous, the morally loose companions of youth
diviners who cast pebbles to tell fortunes

context: One of the greatest wisdom poems (hikma) in Arabic literature, containing some of Labid's most quoted lines — especially 'wealth and family are merely a trust' and 'a man is like a shooting star.' The poem blends stoic acceptance of death with Islamic monotheism in its dismissal of divination, making it a defining work of transitional Jahili-Islamic poetry.

titleAr: يا مَيَّ قُومي في المَآتِمِ وَانْدُبي
titleEn: O Mayy, rise up in the mourning gatherings

verses.sadr: يا مَيَّ قُومي في المَآتِمِ وَانْدُبي
verses.ajuz: فتىً كانَ ممّن يَبتني المَجدَ أرْوَعَا

verses.sadr: وَقُولي: ألا لا يُبْعِدِ اللّهُ أرْبَدَا
عَمِيدُ أُنَاسٍ قَدْ أتَى الدَّهْرُ دونَهُ
verses.ajuz: وَخَطُّوا لهُ يوْمًا منَ الأرْضِ مضْجَعَا

verses.sadr: وكانَ سَبيلَ النّاسِ، مَن كانَ قَبلَهُ
لَعَمْرُ أبيكِ الخَيرِ يا ابنَة َ أرْبَدٍ
فتىً ً عارِفٌ للحَقِّ لا يُنكِرُ القِرَى
لحَا اللّهُ هَذا الدَّهرَ إنّي رَأيْتُهُ
verses.ajuz: بصِيرًا بما سَاءَ ابنَ آدَمَ مُولعًا

translationEn: O Mayy, rise in the mourning gatherings and lament a young man who was among those who built glory with awe-inspiring character. Say: 'God, do not distance Arbad' — and nurse with it the crack in the grief-shattered heart. He was the support of his people, yet time came to him and they traced out for him one day a resting place in the earth. A caller called Arbad — one who always responds — and made him hear; but Arbad could not persist and resist it. It was the path of all people who came before him, and that same force which annihilated Iyad and the Tubba' kings. By the life of your father of good, O daughter of Arbad, a grief has afflicted me that struck and caused pain. The parting of a brother who was my beloved — he slipped away and the treachery of fate carried him off in haste. So my two eyes, since the separation has ruined what was Arbad, do not freeze up — let them flow and weep. He was a young man who knew the right and never refused hospitality — you would see his gift to the guest brimming and overflowing. May God curse this time — I have seen it keenly skilled at whatever causes the son of Adam suffering, passionately devoted to it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to build glory for oneself, to construct renown
awe-inspiring, one who strikes with admiration
the main support, the pillar a people lean on
the treacherous reversal of fate, death's cunning
to freeze, to be dry — said of eyes that cannot weep
a gift, a vessel of food offered to guests
filled to the brim, overflowing

context: An elegy for Arbad addressed to Mayy (a female companion or family member) and to Arbad's daughter. Labid urges weeping and lamentation, praises Arbad's generosity and justice, and closes with a famous curse against Time as an entity that takes pleasure in human suffering — one of the most dramatic closings in his elegiac poetry.

titleEn: I came to Abu Hind with Hind and Malik

verses.ajuz: فجئتُ غِشاشًا إذْ دعتْ أُمُّ طارِقِ

translationEn: I came to Abu Hind with Hind and Malik, and to Asma — for I am among the defenders of what is sacred. She called to me and her eyes overflowed with tears at a camel's watering — and I came in haste when Umm Tariq called. I had prepared an inherited sword of few blemishes, strongly built, which seeks out the path through obstacles. And a worn but celebrated lance whose iron head was battle-tested, and a brown feared spear, noble in tight straits. I left behind there 'Amir, Ibn 'Amir, and 'Amr — and none of me was replaced by a young man on his shoulder. I surpass the leaders in merit and bounty, as the rain of lightning revives the dune-sands. By my life, I said: how can Marthad and 'Amr be left behind while our wealth passes into the hands of distant strangers? Had it not been for my cunning in affairs and my firmness, captives would have been sold for a few scrawny sheep. That was a defense of your father's sacred things when the edge of the elbow-joint tore through the coat of mail.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sacred things, what is inviolably protected — women, honor, property
in haste, quickly (adverb)
an inherited sword passed down through generations
notches or blemishes on a blade
what a man is bound to protect — sacred charges of honor
a coat of mail, armor shirt
firmness, strength of character, mental toughness

context: A poem of self-praise in response to a woman's call for help, in which Labid boasts of his weapons, his military skill, and his defense of the tribe's honor. The poem provides insight into the Jahili concept of the warrior-protector and the sacred duty to defend women and family property at all costs.

titleAr: رَأيتَ ابنَ بَدرٍ ذُلَّ قومِكَ فاعْترِفْ
titleEn: You saw ibn Badr humble your people, confess it

verses.sadr: رَأيتَ ابنَ بَدرٍ ذُلَّ قومِكَ فاعْترِفْ
فإنْ كُنتَ قد سَوَّقتَ مِعْزى حَبَلَّقًا
أبا مالِكٍ إنْ كُنتَ بالسَّيرِ مُعْجَبًا
هُمُ حَيَّةُ الوادي فإنْ كُنتَ راقِيًا
verses.ajuz: فدونَكَ أدْرِكْ ما ازْدهَوْا من فِنائكَا

translationEn: You saw Ibn Badr humiliate your people — admit it — on the morning Jahsh shot Malik with a broken-tipped arrow. He was the best among you in soul and in father — the mightiest of them, cherished while alive and mourned when dead. You recalled a need you had forgotten with him, and at the pool there is another need waiting behind you. If you have herded a flock of small measly goats, Abu Malik — call your thorn-gatherers to you. Abu Malik, if you are so impressed by your travels — come, look into the eyes of your own women. Abu Malik, I am one who rejects your judgment, and Zabban too has become one who rejects your judgment by evening. They are the viper of the valley — if you are a snake-charmer, then come and reclaim what they have taken from your enclosure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to confess, to acknowledge what has happened
an arrow whose notch is broken — a bad weapon
a pool of rainwater in a rocky hollow
small and thin goats of poor quality
to call to animals, to herd them with cries
one who rejects, who refuses to accept something
the viper of the valley — metaphor for a dangerous, implacable enemy

context: A satirical poem of reproach against a man called Abu Malik, challenging his authority and judgment while defending Labid's own position. The closing image of 'the viper of the valley' — referring to Labid's allies as a dangerous force — gives the poem its fierce edge.

titleEn: Did you not pass by the ancient ruins
translationEn: Did you not pause at the ancient ruins of Salma's encampments by the watercourses and then al-Qufal, along the two sides of Saw'ar and the valleys of Qaww — ruins that endure without speaking of departure? The people moved on, leaving only the braying of wild asses and the sound of strings after the inhabited communities. And a thread of henna-stained she-camels clustered together, their young resembling the dappled foals of small camels. The people departed and the summer gazelles took over the shaded tents. I lingered there until my companions said: you are overwrought, and that is no boon. His tears were like two water-wheels whose attendants pour bucket upon bucket, watering a crop and fodder-plants before tipping the buckets onto tall lean she-camels. The poem then moves to a meditation on longing and the resolution to cut emotional ties and journey on a powerful she-camel. This transitions into an extended description of a wild bull-oryx sheltering at night in a tamarisk thicket, sleeping in the rain, hunted at dawn by eager hounds — he turns and fights them back nobly. Then comes a second nature set-piece: a wild ass and his harem navigating summer heat, finding stagnant watering holes, leading the herd to a distant flood-watering. Then a magnificent storm sequence: lightning observed from a distance, clouds like Ethiopian warriors with spears and swords, rain descending on the tribe's grazing lands. The poem ends with Labid's ambivalence toward his own tribespeople — he loves them yet must criticize the corrupt among them who oppress the innocent and follow the debauched without scruple.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, traces of former campsites (pl. of دِمنة)
the braying of wild asses — a sound of desolation
a herd of wild cows or oryx
a large water-wheel bucket
a heavy pouring rain
hooves (of horses or camels)
moral violations, grave indecencies

context: Labid's longest surviving poem, a grand qasida in al-Wafir meter containing 60 verses. It begins with the classic nasib over abandoned ruins, moves through a meditation on longing, followed by three extended nature descriptions — an oryx hunted by dogs, a wild ass guiding his herd to water, and a rainstorm over the tribe's lands — before closing with tribal praise mixed with reproach of the corrupt. It is considered one of the masterpieces of Jahili poetry.

titleEn: Kubaysha settled at 'Aqil after your time

verses.ajuz: وكانَتْ لهُ خَبْلًا على النّأيِ خابِلا

verses.sadr: على أنَّ ألْواحًا تُرَى في جَديلِها

verses.ajuz: وقد زايلَ البُهمى سَفا العِرْبِ ناصِلا
منَ الوَقعِ لا ضَحْلًا وَلا مُتضَائِلا
وَمِنْ دَحْلَ لا يخشَى بهنَّ الحَبائِلا

verses.sadr: وَقَد زَوَّدَتْ مِنا على النّأيِ حاجَةً
verses.ajuz: وَشَوْقًا لوَ انَّ الشَوْقَ أصْبحَ عادِلا

verses.sadr: يَرُضْنَ صِعَابَ الدُّرِّ في كلِّ حِجَّةٍ

verses.ajuz: منَ النّاصعِ المَختومِ مِن خَمرِ بابلا
سَنًا رَصَفًا مِنْ آخِرِ اللّيلِ سائِلا
سمعتَ لها من واكِفِ العُطبِ وَاشِلا

verses.sadr: فإنْ تَنَأ دارٌ أو يَطُلْ عَهْدُ خُلَّةٍ
مَدَى العَينِ مِنْها أنْ يُراعَ بنَجْوَة ٍ
تَلُومُ على الإهْلاكِ في غَيرِ ضَلَّةٍ
verses.ajuz: وهَلْ لي ما أمسكْتُ إنْ كنتُ باخِلا
رَباحًا إذا ما المَرْءُ أصْبَحَ ثَاقِلا

verses.sadr: وهَلْ هوَ إلَاّ ما ابتَنَى في حَياتِهِ
فَدَعْ عَنْكَ هذا قد مَضَى لسبيلِهِ
verses.ajuz: وكَلَّفْ نجيَّ الهَمِّ إنْ كنتَ رَاحِلا
وأُبْرئُ هَمًّا كانَ في الصَّدرِ داخلا

verses.sadr: وإنْ آتِهِ أصْرِفْ إذا خِفتُ نَبوَة ً
verses.ajuz: وأحبسْ قَلوصَ الشُّحِّ إن كانَ باخِلا

verses.sadr: بنُو عامرٍ منْ خَيرِ حَيٍّ عَلِمتُهمْ
لهُمْ مَجلِسٌ لا يحصَرُونَ عن النَّدى
verses.ajuz: ولا يزْدَهيهمْ جَهلُ من كانَ جاهلا

verses.sadr: وَبِيضٌ على النّيرانِ في كلِّ شَتوَةٍ

verses.ajuz: وَلَمْ يَكُ ساعِينَا عَنِ المَجْدِ غافِلا

verses.sadr: وَلَنْ يَعدَمُوا في الحَرْبِ لَيثًا مُجرَّبًا
صَبَرْنا لَهُمْ في كُلِّ يَوْمِ عَظيمَةٍ
وإنْ تسألُوا عنهُمْ لدى كلِّ غارةٍ
verses.ajuz: فَقَدْ يُنْبأُ الأخبارَ مَنْ كانَ سائِلا
وقد يُخبَرُ الأنْباءَ مَن كانَ جَاهِلا

translationEn: Kubaysha settled at 'Aqil after you last knew it, and it had been for her a source of obsessive longing and enchantment across the distance. She spent spring on the highlands and summer at the broad flat lowlands, and the herds grazed the wadis between al-Rijam and Wasit down to the lotus trees of al-Rassayn. Doves sing atop it every morning on the acacia trees, singing through midmorning and the late afternoons. The poet then sets out on a strong she-camel described in vivid detail, traveling through wild desert terrain. The camel section gives way to the description of a wild bull-oryx that grazes in the cloud-watered plains all summer, then is driven by the summer heat from his pools, forced to migrate through mountain passes to a distant water source — guided by the male wild ass, the scene interweaving with descriptions of the she-asses' gestation and the onager's lament-like braying compared to the lament of a drinker who has been locked out of the Babylonian wine-jar. The oryx watering scene is magnificent: herd after herd arriving, the watering hole churning. Then the lightning and rain scene again with thunder-clouds like Ethiopian warriors. The poem then shifts to the women of the tribe in their litters, richly adorned with pearls and coral, riding on decorated mounts. A woman who has grown older reproaches Labid for spending his wealth too freely; he replies that piety and praise are the best trade, and what a man builds in life is what remains when the stones are laid on his grave. The poem ends with a long and celebratory passage of tribal fakhr praising the Banu 'Amir for their generosity, hospitality, military valor, and noble customs — among the finest such passages in Labid's corpus. The closing refrain 'those are my people' rings through its final verses.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: obsessive enchantment, madness caused by love or longing
late afternoons, the hours before sunset
a large powerful she-camel — also thought, imagination
milch camels with full udders, laden with milk
boulders, large stones placed over a grave
piety and praise — Labid's Islamic formulation of true wealth
the destitute who travel seeking alms or charity

context: Labid's longest surviving poem at 92 verses, considered by many critics his masterpiece. It follows the classic qasida structure — nasib (love prelude over ruins), riḥla (journey on a she-camel), wasf al-thiyara (description of wild animals: bull-oryx and onager), description of the beloved's caravan departing, and an extended section of tribal fakhr — with extraordinary richness and technical mastery throughout.

titleEn: Whose ruins does Uthal embrace
translationEn: Whose ruins does Uthal embrace, then Sarhadha, then al-Marana, then al-Khayal? Then Nab'u and al-Nabi' and Dhu Sudayr — where the gazelles of the she-oryx have their fawns? I was reminded by them of the horsemen and the drinking companions, and my eyes' tears poured and streamed. As if I were in the assembly of the Banu Uqaysh — when you enter their gathering you are struck with awe. In it, Qurzzul and al-Jawn were plentiful, and Tahjul and al-Na'ama and al-Khabal — all famous prized horses. These are the remnants of a heritage left by the leaders of old and what the heavy rains of spring had gathered.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, traces of a former encampment
to contain, to embrace within its boundaries
white gazelles, oryx gazelles
fawns, the young of gazelles or oryx
drinking companions, intimate friends (pl. of نَديم)
pouring down, flowing freely (of tears or rain)
early spring rains, the heavy showers of Rabi'

context: A short nasib poem of six verses lamenting the ruins of former encampments at a series of named places, the sight of which reminds Labid of noble horsemen and companions now gone. The mention of famous horses by name (Qurzzul, al-Jawn, etc.) suggests a gathering of tribal glory now only preserved in memory.

titleEn: The ruins told nothing of their people

verses.sadr: لَيْسَ فيها ما إنْ يُبَيِّنُ للسّا

translationEn: The ruins revealed nothing of their people — years upon years have passed since they were last inhabited. Nothing remains in them to tell the questioner except young gazelles and ostrich chicks. And the reaching dun-colored she-oryxes resting at al-Sullan — solitary ones and herds. And a dark-faced wild bull pursuing a yearling she-oryx — a cave-born, scarred, clay-hued bull. And a she-wolf seeking her pact at Harba, from a spotted hyena whose resting place has been claimed by the maddening jinn. She kept vigil over that pact and spent the night upon it between Falj and al-Lawdh — gray fierce she-wolves. For three days she sought it at the two sands, with anguish daily in her breast. Then she met — after despair — a keen-eyed thing, and a skin with some of its joints still attached.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins, traces left by a departed tribe
young gazelles, fawns (pl. of جُؤذَر)
young ostriches, ostrich chicks
she-oryxes that reach up with their necks to browse
dun-colored, tawny (of wild oryxes or camels)
clay-colored, the color of sun-dried mud
anguish, distressing confusion of the heart

context: A short poem beginning with the classic motif of uninformative ruins, quickly shifting to vivid desert wildlife scenes: oryxes, a wild bull pursuing a yearling, and most strikingly a she-wolf seeking her prey — an unusual choice that gives the poem a distinctive dark naturalism. The closing image of finding a partial carcass after days of searching is characteristically stark and unsentimental.
